title: 2022-ADJ-Academy-Comprensive-PR (Part 1)
text:
Program Review 2020-2022: Law Enforcement Academy
Prepared by: Abner Weed
1. Description of Program:
The Law Enforcement Academy state mandated curriculum which prepares students for
law enforcement careers in a variety of criminal justice fields at the local, state, and
national levels. The program is designed to instruct students on the principles of the
federal, state, and local justice systems and subsystems. Emphasis is placed on
practical exposure to Crime Scene Investigation, Criminal Law, Criminal Investigations,
Forensics, Emergency Management, Homeland Security, Principles of Criminal
Defense, Community Relations, Corrections, Firearms Training, Juvenile Law, Arrest
and Control, Emergency Vehicle Operations, and California Laws. The academy
program at the college dovetails into an Associate of Arts degree or an Associate in
Science degree for transfer to a California State University. The provides required
perishable skills and advanced officer training courses for the Law Enforcement
Community.
a) Organization: The ADJ Program is under direction of the Dean of Career and
Technical Education. The ADJ faculty include one full-time Coordinator of the
Law Enforcement Academy, several adjunct faculty teaching in the Academy,
and one full-time faculty teaching the degree curriculum.
b) Primary purpose: The primary purpose of the program is to prepare students for
careers in criminal justice fields, increase their knowledge and capabilities as
officers and offer them incentives toward promotional opportunities gained with
further education.
c) Whom you serve: We serve all students interested in criminal justice careers
and provide curriculum to assist those already in the law enforcement fields to
obtain their degree and maintain their perishable skills.
d) Services provided: We offer a modular format Law Enforcement Academy and
curriculum leading to degrees and a Certificate of Achievement. We also offer
perishable skills training and host non-typical courses.
e) How services are provided:
a. Face to face Academy
b. Degree applicable coursework
c. Transfer courses
d. Face to face vocational courses through the regional training center
f) Describe how curriculum is up to date and needs based: Curriculum for the
Law Enforcement Academy is reviewed and updated by P.O.S.T. (Peace Officer
Standards and Training) as needed. Those changes are reflected in our
mandated courses and approved by P.O.S.T. before each academy starts. The

-- 1 of 11 --

title: 2022-ADJ-Academy-Comprensive-PR (Part 2)
text:
curriculum for the degree and COA are updated every two years, as required.
The curriculum is aligned with the ADJ Programs of several north-state
community colleges, and fulfills the needs of those interested in careers in the
criminal justice system.
g) Breakdown of classes offered:
1) In July 1993, the Commission adopted training specifications as its method of
specifying the minimum, mandated curriculum for the Regular Basic Course.
Listed below are the individual learning domains that make up the course
curriculum.
• LD 01 Leadership, Professionalism and Ethics
• LD 02 Criminal Justice System
• LD 03 Principled Policing in the Community
• LD 04 Victimology/Crisis Intervention
• LD 05 Introduction to Criminal Law
• LD 06 Property Crimes
• LD 07 Crimes Against Persons
• LD 08 General Criminal Statutes
• LD 09 Crimes Against Children
• LD 10 Sex Crimes
• LD 11 Juvenile Law and Procedure
• LD 12 Controlled Substances
• LD 13 ABC Law
• LD 15 Laws of Arrest
• LD 16 Search and Seizure
• LD 17 Presentation of Evidence
• LD 18 Investigative Report Writing
• LD 19 Vehicle Operations
• LD 20 Use of Force/De-escalation
• LD 21 Patrol Techniques
• LD 22 Vehicle Pullovers
• LD 23 Crimes in Progress
• LD 24 Handling Disputes/Crowd Control
• LD 25 Domestic Violence
• LD 26 Critical Incidents
• LD 27 Missing Persons
• LD 28 Traffic Enforcement
• LD 29 Traffic Accident Investigation
• LD 30 Crime Scenes, Evidence, and Forensics
• LD 31 Custody

-- 2 of 11 --

• LD 32 Lifetime Fitness
• LD 33 Arrest and Control
• LD 34 First Aid and CPR (doc)
• LD 35 Firearms/Chemical Agents (doc)
• LD 36 Information Systems (doc)
• LD 37 People with Disabilities (doc)
• LD 38 Gang Awareness (doc)
• LD 39 Crimes Against the Justice System (doc)
• LD 40 Weapons Violations (doc)
• LD 42 Cultural Diversity/Discrimination (doc)
• LD 43 Terrorism Awareness (doc)
• 2) The Northern California Regional Training Center Provides Peace Officer
Standards and Training (P.O.S.T.) as well as California Board of State and
Community Corrections (S.T.C) certified vocational, perishable skills and
transfer academy as listed below:
• BASIC COURSE - MODULE I (INT)
• BASIC COURSE - MODULE II (INT)
• BASIC COURSE - MODULE III (INT)
• CRISIS INTERVENTION BEHAV HEALTH TRAINING FTO
• DRIVING(PSP)
• FIREARM(PSP)
• FIREARMS/TACTICAL RIFLE
• FIRST AID/CPR/AED REFRESHER
• PC 832 - ARREST AND FIREARMS
• PC 832 ARREST
• PC 832 FIREARMS
• POST MULTI-MEDIA COURSE
• SCENARIO EVALUATOR TRAINING
• USE OF FORCE (PSP)
2. External factors impacting program

-- 3 of 11 --

title: 2022-ADJ-Academy-Comprensive-PR (Part 3)
text:
a) Budgetary constraints or dedicated classroom opportunities: Inadequate budget
development relating to the Police Academy and Regional Training Center;
inadequate facilities for future program growth.
b) Competition from other colleges;
Nearest law enforcement academies do compete with our police academy (e.g.,
Butte, Redwoods, Sacramento, etc.). Degree programs – online offerings offer direct
competition to other institutions.
a) University requirements: None.
b) Institutional regulation, policies, mandates, etc. None noted.
c) Non-institutional regulations, polices, mandates, etc.: Peace Officer Standards and
Training (POST) has direct oversight over the Law Enforcement Academy training
standards. Dynamic nature of law requires bi-annual updates to course work for all
courses.
a. Requirements of employers: local law enforcement agencies have been
hiring our academy cadets who meet the minimum entry-level
requirements and have earned mandated certifications.
b. Developments in the field (current and former): New technologies, law
changes especially in the area of criminal investigations have required
curriculum updates. New criminal laws and arrest and control have also
required curriculum updates. The move from simulator based training to
practical skills based training has required the update of P.O.S.T.
required curriculum, training modalities and equipment purchases.
Other: A lack of significant professional development funding requires
faculty to maintain currency in the field and learn about new technologies
on their own. P.O.S.T. is the only regulatory entity pertaining to law
enforcement training standards.
d) Job Market
a. Law Enforcement Academy meets the needs of prospective employers.
b. Academy prepares students for entry-level employment in law enforcement.
c. Development in the field –changing technologies and educational requirements
affect recruitment and retention for this job market.
2. Progress on Outcomes Assessment
a) SLOs evaluated bi-annually and evaluated for alignment with programmatic SLOs
b) None
c) Continual program and course assessment throughout next four years
3 . Progress on Outcomes Assessment

-- 4 of 11 --

title: 2022-ADJ-Academy-Comprensive-PR (Part 4)
text:
a) Summarize progress on SLO measures applied since last review: SLO’s are
formulated from P.O.S.T Training and Testing Specifications which are updated
prior to the start of an academy in an expanded course outline to ensure
coverage of state mandated material.
b) Program, course, instructional improvements made as a result of outcomes
assessment: All curriculum updated and approved by P.O.S.T. prior to start of
each academy.
c) Program assessment plan for next two years: Continuous monitoring of case law,
Industry standards and technology as well as mandated curriculum updates.

b) Course success rate: Out of the 118 students graduating our program 96 obtained
employment. Our program success for placing students in the workforce as law
enforcement officers is 81%. The increasing trend of agencies sending sponsored
cadets will increase this number. The imbedded job placement activities have helped
recruitment and hiring while in academy.
1. 2020, 1st academy - 27 graduated, 20 working in law enforcement.
2. 2020, 2 nd academy - 13 graduated, 09 working in law enforcement.
3. 2020 832 P.C. - 14 graduated, 14 working in law enforcement
4. 2021 1 st academy - 12 graduated, 11 working in law enforcement.

• 2020-22 ADJ 9079 - Northern Regional Training Center offering P.O.S.T. advanced
officer and perishable skills trained 932 individuals with 100% pass rate.
• This distinct training benefit to local agencies is a welcome financial infusion in to
the campus. As we continue to move toward incorporating more ongoing and refresher
training this trend will continue as we develop the program offerings.
c) FT/PT faculty ratio – FT/PT Faculty ratio has not been found to affect student
success; currently there is one FT Faculty instructor and approximately 25 PT
subject matter expert instructors for the law enforcement academy.
The limitations noted have been in support staff necessary to upkeep, track and
repair the equipment necessary.
.
d) Other information collected was intermingled with San Francisco Police Department,
who still runs some of its’ own Advanced Officer Courses, results were not indicative
of our program.

text:
4. Other Unit-Specific Quantitative and Qualitative Research
a) Certification Information
Students receiving a certificate or Industry certification or license Year

b) Based upon the above data tables, students are achieving certifications and degrees
at above-average rates
c) Evaluation of these measures indicate students have found gainful employment in
the field.
5. Evaluation
a) PERKINS summary
o Individuals with disabilities- very low numbers in the program
o Economically disadvantaged- as a percentage of the overall cohort, used to be a
majority, but now significantly less as a percentage. Could be due to SF Police
Academy. Still within State negotiated levels, however.
o Non-traditional (women)
 Women represent around 20% of the cohort, within the state negotiated
rate
o English-language learners- no data
o Homeless- no data
o Out-of-work- very low numbers in the program
o Former foster care youth- no data
Single parents- very low numbers in the program
b) Online offerings and evening courses will improve student success in obtaining A.S.
Degree.
c) Partnerships with local law enforcement agencies within the service area are robust.
d) Best practices are updated and implemented as the dynamic of the law and
emerging training needs are recognized.
e) Frustration in lack of facilities for the growth of the academic program and law
enforcement academy. Space needs on campus are a constant struggle to find high
intensity, short duration spaces on campus such as the JCO-PO Transfer Academy
at a full 2 weeks straight. Most classroom spaces are scheduled on a limited intensity
and use the semester duration. They seemed to be scheduled 1-2 hours a day. 1-2
times a week.
f) Staffing needs are currently not met because certain academy staff positions remain
unfilled (Coordinator, Clerical). There are plans in process to rebalance that should

-- 9 of 11 --

title: 2022-ADJ-Academy-Comprensive-PR (Part 8)
text:
be completed in January. There are still needs for a Fleet manager and STC content
generator for other class types.
g) Robust Advisory Committee that meets bi-annually to discuss planning and decision-
making of the program; faculty participate in institutional shared governance.
h) Faculty flex opportunities have met some professional development needs; however,
the changing law enforcement profession and dynamic nature of the law require
faculty, on their own time, to pursue professional development opportunities outside
their work schedules. The intensive time required to operate and supervise the
academy effectively does not allow FLEX opportunities in the same was as other
instructors in other disciplines.
i) Law Enforcement Academy complies with the California Commission on POST; CTE
program reviews and course updates are completed every two years.
6. Vision
In the future we hope to have a dedicated training facility for our academic, police academy, and
advanced officer training programs.
Because the Administration of Justice Program has such a robust program, we would like to
have enough room to simultaneously offer courses and ensure future growth of the program.
We would like to hire adequate staffing for the program and send instructors to conferences and
trainings to update their skills, this will keep the program complaint with P.O.S.T. standards.
We would like to also like to increase course offerings to include;
1) Basic dispatcher academy.
2) Dispatcher update training.
3) Combined Police/Fire/Medical training session and practical exercise events.
3) Cross certifying our current P.O.S.T. approved courses to meet STC standards.
7. Progress on Prior Goals
a) Prior goals to establish a location on campus for the program to grow met
immediate needs at the time, however in the meantime, we have
exceeded the limits of our available space. A dedicated or Public Safety
Muli-use facility would be optimal for growth of program
b) On line ADJ courses are helping to facilitate A.S. Degree completion for
graduates and future students.
8. Four-Year Action Plan and Continuous Quality Improvement Proposal
